title: Choosing a brokerage when you plan to lease
dek: Most brokerage advice is written for sales agents. Leasing agents need different answers.
heroAlt: Choosing a brokerage when you plan to lease

blocks.html: New agents pick brokerages on brand names and splits. Reasonable, if you plan to sell houses. If you plan to build a leasing business, the standard checklist misses the questions that will actually shape your first year.

A leasing-friendly brokerage is not just one that allows rentals. It is one where rentals are respected: where the paperwork flow is built for lease velocity, where commissions get processed in days not months, and where nobody in leadership treats your fifteen deals a month like a warm-up act.

blocks.text: The wrong brokerage will not stop you. It will just quietly tax every deal you do.

items.title: How fast do lease commissions pay out?
items.html: Leasing runs on velocity. A brokerage that takes sixty days to process a rental check breaks your cash flow model.

items.title: Who here leases at volume?
items.html: If nobody does, you will be inventing every process alone. One producing leasing agent in the office is worth more than any training program.

items.title: What does the split buy me?
items.html: A higher split with zero support can beat a famous brand, or lose to one. Price the resources, not the logo.

blocks.html: Book conversations with three brokerages this week and ask all three questions at each. Take notes. The differences will be louder than any recruiting pitch.
Course 1 walks the full decision with an interview checklist, and the free e-book frames where the brokerage call fits in your first ninety days.

blocks.lead: The e-book covers the full foundation, including this call.
blocks.label: Get the Free E-Book
